coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast synthesis of gold nanosphere cluster coated by graphene quantum dot for active targeting PA/CT imaging and near-infrared laser/pH-triggered chemo-photothermal synergistic tumor therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Xuan, Yang
Zhang, Ruo-Yun
Zhao, Dong-Hui
Zhang, Xiao-Shuai
An, Jie
Cheng, Kai
Hou, Xiao-Lin
Song, Xian-Lin
Zhao, Yuan-Di
Yang, Xiao-Quan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, simple raw materials and reaction conditions were used to synthesize a versatile nanoprobe using a one-step method. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) and gold chloride were mixed and irradiated with ultra-violet (UV) radiation for 1 min. Then, the gold nanosphere cluster with the diameter of 50 nm and coated using GQD was formed using a facile one-step approach. GQD played the roles of reducing agent, stabilizer and drug carrier instead of a harmful reducing agent or stabilizer. The nanoprobe had good dispersion, stability, excellent photoacoustic imaging (PAI) and computed tomography (CT) imaging performance, low cytotoxicity and photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 51.31%. The results for cell and animal experiments showed that targeted PAI/CT imaging of tumor after modification of folic acid (FA) could be obtained using the probe. Meanwhile, after the adsorption of doxorubicin, the chemo-photothermal combined therapy for tumor could be carried out through controlled drug release from GQD under heated and acidic environment of tumor. Additionally, the treatment effect was significantly superior to single modes. The body weight, Hematoxylin and Eeosin (H&E) staining of main organs and blood biochemical indicators showed that the probe had good biological safety after injection. The current work proposes a new dual-mode bio-imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy nanoprobe, which presents good application prospect for tumor theragnostic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoacoustic imaging
CT imaging
Chemo-photothermal therapy
Drug release
One-step synthesis

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro targeted dual-mode imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy
In vivo dual-mode imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy
PAI and CT evaluation of probe
Encapsulation efficiency, loading efficiency and release rate of Au/DOX@GQD-FA
In vitro PAI, CT and laser scanning confocal image
In vivo PAI and CT imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process of Au/DOX@GQD-FA, targeting dual-modal PAI/CT and chemo-photothermal combined therapy for tumor.

TEM of GQD (A) and Au@GQD (B, C and D). Inset in C (from top to bottom): GQD mixed with gold chloride, GQD mixed with gold chloride and irradiated with UV for 1 min, gold chloride irradiated with UV for 1 min; absorption spectra of traditional gold nanosphere, DOX, Au@GQD and Au/DOX@GQD (E); absorption spectra of Au/DOX@GQD-FA and FA (F), insert: absorption spectrum of Au/DOX@GQD-FA and traditional gold nanosphere; XRD diffraction spectrum of Au@GQD (G); dynamic light scattering results of Au@GQD, n = 5 (H); zeta potentials of Au@GQD, Au/DOX@GQD and Au/DOX@GQD-FA, n = 5 (I); changes in size and zeta potential of Au@GQD at 37 °C, n = 5 (J); different masses of DOX corresponding to encapsulation efficiency and loading efficiency for 1 g Au@GQD, n = 5 (K); DOX release efficiency of Au/DOX@GQD-FA under different pH (L) and laser (pH = 7.4) (M), n = 3; ** : p < 0.01.

Changes in temperature of 1.0 mg/mL Au@GQD using different power densities of laser over time (A, B); changes in temperature of different concentrations of Au@GQD under 1.0 W/cm2 laser (C, D); thermal stability of probe (E) and photothermal conversion efficiency (F).

PA response of different concentrations of Au@GQD (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mg/mL) (A), inset: Au@GQD PAI; CT response of different concentrations of Au@GQD (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 and 40 mg/mL) and iohexol (B), inset: Au@GQD CT imaging; PA response (C), PAI (D), CT (E) and laser confocal imaging (F) of HeLa and A549 cells treated using different probes, ** : p < 0.01.

Cytotoxicity of Au@GQD-FA as determined through MTT experiments (A), n = 5; survival rate of HeLa cells with different drug concentrations and different treatments through MTT experiments (B), n = 6, **: p < 0.01; percent of hemolytic red blood cell after being incubated with Au@GQD at various concentrations for different times (C, inset: a digital photograph of hemolytic result); HeLa and A549 cells irradiated using the laser or without irradiation after incubation with Au@GQD-FA and Au@GQD, respectively, white line: the edge of laser (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PAI of tumor site of HeLa tumor-burdened nude mouse before and after probe injection under 744 nm wavelength excitation (A), CT imaging before and after 0.25, 24, 48 and 75 h of intratumor injection (B); thermal imaging of tumor-burdened nude mice after injecting PBS, DOX, Au@GQD-FA and Au/DOX@GQD-FA at different times (C) and changes in temperature with time (D), n = 6; body weight curve (E), tumor growth curve (F), survival rate (G) after different treatments; n = 5, **: p < 0.05, *: p < 0.01.
Photographs of mice after different treatments and H&E staining result of tumor site after 6 h treatment.

Time-dependent biodistribution of Au/DOX@GQD-FA after tail vein injection (A), n = 3; blood sample analysis after injecting PBS and probe: WBC, RBC, HGB, PLT, ALT, AST, BUN and ALP (B-I), n = 5; H&E staining results for main organs (J).

head.ce:title: Ultrafast synthesis of gold nanosphere cluster coated by graphene quantum dot for active targeting PA/CT imaging and near-infrared laser/pH-triggered chemo-photothermal synergistic tumor therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Britton Chance Center for Biomedical Photonics at Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics – Hubei Bioinformatics & Molecular Imaging Key Laboratory, Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science and Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, Hubei, PR China

Zhao).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Britton Chance Center for Biomedical Photonics at Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics – Hubei Bioinformatics & Molecular Imaging Key Laboratory
College of Life Science and Technology
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, simple raw materials and reaction conditions were used to synthesize a versatile nanoprobe using a one-step method. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) and gold chloride were mixed and irradiated with ultra-violet (UV) radiation for 1 min. Then, the gold nanosphere cluster with the diameter of 50 nm and coated using GQD was formed using a facile one-step approach. GQD played the roles of reducing agent, stabilizer and drug carrier instead of a harmful reducing agent or stabilizer. The nanoprobe had good dispersion, stability, excellent photoacoustic imaging (PAI) and computed tomography (CT) imaging performance, low cytotoxicity and photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 51.31%. The results for cell and animal experiments showed that targeted PAI/CT imaging of tumor after modification of folic acid (FA) could be obtained using the probe. Meanwhile, after the adsorption of doxorubicin, the chemo-photothermal combined therapy for tumor could be carried out through controlled drug release from GQD under heated and acidic environment of tumor. Additionally, the treatment effect was significantly superior to single modes. The body weight, Hematoxylin and Eeosin (H&E) staining of main organs and blood biochemical indicators showed that the probe had good biological safety after injection. The current work proposes a new dual-mode bio-imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy nanoprobe, which presents good application prospect for tumor theragnostic.

ce:section.ce:para: Early diagnosis of a tumor significantly increases the curing rate of a patient, and therefore, is of great significance. Previously, a number of imaging methods have been used in tumor’s diagnosis, including ultrasound imaging (UI), fluorescence imaging (FI), magnetic resonance imaging, positron emission computed tomography, computed tomography (CT), and photoacoustic imaging (PAI) [1]. Among these methods, CT is a commonly used method in diagnostics due to its deep tissue penetration and low cost. Unfortunately, some early cancers could not be diagnosed accurately using CT technology because of its poor sensitivity. Meanwhile, researchers were conscious of PAI [2,3], which was a novel biomedical imaging method with non-ionizing and non-invasive advantages and combined high penetration depth of UI and excellent contrast of FI. PAI could reduce light scattering in imaging quality, surmount the limit of depth in FI, and develop deep tissue image with high resolution and sensitivity [4]. Therefore, the combination of CT and PAI is an ideal dual-modal imaging technology, in which, CT provides spatial information of deep tissue, while PAI gives the location and distribution of trace tumor-related molecules in the tissue. Then, highly sensitive molecular information about the disease and high-resolution pathological image can be obtained simultaneously [5]. Enhanced CT imaging is based on some iodine compounds, however these small molecules would be quickly cleaned by kidneys due to their low molecular weights [6]. Therefore, the method is unfit for targeting cancer imaging. Due to this reason, a novel CT contrast agent is eagerly sought. Some recent studies suggest that certain metallic nanoparticles presented better contrast for CT than iodine compounds due to their high atomic numbers (Bi, 83; Au, 79; Ta, 73 and I, 53) and X-ray attenuation coefficients (Bi, 5.74; Au, 5.16; Ta, 4.30 and I, 1.94 cm2/g at 100 keV), and therefore, theoretically, provide better CT imaging effect than iodine [7,8].

Photothermal therapy (PTT) has attracted a lot of research attention as an efficacious therapy for tumors in recent years [9–13]. During the process, near-infrared (NIR) light is used to increase the temperature of tumor site to efficacious therapy temperature (>41 °C). The temperature is maintained at this value for some time. Cancer cells are killed due to high temperature, while the normal cells remain safe because of their high temperature tolerance. Although NIR light has excellent tissues penetration, it is difficult for it to penetrate deep tissues and therefore, heat cannot accumulate enough to kill the tumor [14]. Resent clinical researches have showed that, beside direct lethal effect on cancer, PTT could also enhance the therapeutic effect of chemotherapy and inhibit cancer recurrence and metastasis in combined therapy. It is considered that heat also helps chemotherapy drug enter tumor tissue and increase cytotoxic effect [15]. This dual-modal combined therapy has attracted much research attention in recent years.

PTT was used in the treatment of chemo-photothermal combined therapy to accurately release drug molecules and photothermal agent to cancer and selectively, kill cancer cells. Nanocarriers are one of the main ways to meet this need. In this model, contrast agent, drug and photothermal agent are loaded in a stimulus response type nanocarrier. When the nanocarrier arrives the tumor site, it will release drug molecules in a controlled way due to change in various environmental factors (such as pH, temperature, reducing material, light and enzyme) [16]. On the other hand, PTT is carried out under the guidance of image technology using a contrast agent. This helps in achieving the aim of combined therapy [17,18].

Gold nanospheres are widely used in biomedical research. In addition, several kinds of gold nanoparticles having special properties were developed (gold nanorod, gold nanocage, gold nanostar, and gold nanoshell) [19]. These novel gold nanoparticles not only have the same level of ability for CT imaging as of gold nanospheres, but also have the abilities of PAI and PTT. Due to these reasons, they are regarded as multifunctional nanomaterials. In a previous work, preliminary work was conducted to design a new multifunctional nanoprobe GMS/DOX@SLB-FA, whose core consisted of gold nanostar, while the shell consisted of doxorubicin (DOX)-loaded mesoporous silica. The outside of the nanoprobe was coated with folic acid (FA)-modified thermosensitive supported lipid bilayer, which played the role of gatekeeper. The results showed that the probe exhibited the ability of PAI/CT imaging of tumor, targeted drug release and PTT to simultaneously achieve imaging and treatment [20]. However, these novel, multi-functional gold nanoparticles have complicated synthesis steps, poisonous nature and harmful reagent residues, which have restricted their practical applications. Due to these drawbacks, developing a new type of gold nanoparticles is important.

Traditional small gold nanosphered have no photo-thermal effect in NIR area as they do not absorb light. Only when the diameter is big enough, gold nanospheres can strongly absorb NIR light, thus becoming useful for PTT and PAI. Gao’s group reported photolabile gold nanospheres, which were covalently cross-linkable after 405 nm laser irradiation through diazirine terminal group of PEG5000 ligand on the surface of gold nanospheres (20.5 nm). Additionally, the diameter eventually reached the value of 346 nm. A new absorption peak appeared in the NIR region to achieve tumor PAI and PTT using passive targeting [21]. However, this method was still complicated and needed to introduce a variety of coupling agents. It also could not be combined with chemotherapy drug. Therefore, the preparation method for new gold nanospheres still needs further research.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are a kind of quasi-zero-dimensional carbon materials derived from graphene. Owing to special edge effect and surface structure, these materials show special physical and chemical properties. Their low toxicity, good biocompatibility [22,23], water-solubility and surface characteristics, such as easy to modify, have attracted much attention for applications in the field of biomedical engineering. There are large amounts of reactive functional groups on GQD’s surface, which can be easily modified with targeting molecule (such as FA, RGD and antibodies). GQD has large surface area, and its delocalized π-bond can form specific π-π conjugated structure with aromatic hydrocarbons, making GQD carry drugs as a carrier. Therefore, GQDs’ ability of loading drug has also garnered a lot of research interest. Qiu’s group used RGD coupled GQD to load DOX. This targeting probe could release drugs in the acidic environment of tumor [24]. Beside their advantages, GQDs have the character of photocatalytic reduction, through which, they can catalyze other substances in the system under the irradiation of a certain wavelength. Researchers can take advantage of this characteristic to obtain metal elements, such as gold, and silver [25,26].

In the current work, a very simple and novel method has been established to synthesize a multi-functional nanoprobe. GQD and gold chloride were irradiated with UV for 1 min. Then, the gold nanosphere cluster with 50 nm diameter and coated with GQD was formed (Au@GQD), while GQD played the roles of reducing agent, stabilizer and drug carrier rather than a harmful reducing agent or stabilizer. The gold nanosphere cluster exhibited good photothermal performance due to good absorption in NIR region. On the other hand, it was decorated with FA as the targeting molecule and used as carrier for drugs. Finally, a multi-functional nanoprobe having the abilities of targeting dual-modal imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy for tumor was obtained (Fig. 1 ). The cell and animal results indicated that targeting PAI/CT of cancer could be carried out using the synthesized probe because of the existence of FA. Meanwhile, it presented chemo-photothermal combined therapy for cancer through controlled drug delivery from GQD using NIR laser and in the acidic environment of tumor. The improvement of efficiency and accuracy of drug release was due to the dual control, while the treatment effect was significantly superior to single, individual modes. This work provided a new direction for integrated diagnosis and treatment probes, which present good application prospects for tumor diagnosis and therapy.

It was known that GQD presented good reductive [26], in this paper, it was used as a reducing agent and stabilizer, which ensured effective catalytic activity and good dispersity to obtain Au@GQD. And UV light could promote electron transfer in the reduction process [27]. The results showed that GQD [28] and gold chloride were irradiated with UV for 1 min, after which Au@GQD was formed. TEM results showed that the diameter of GQD was about 5 nm (Fig. 2 A), while that of Au@GQD was about 50 nm (Fig. 2B). Additionally, the outside coated GQD could be seen clearly, and had the thickness of less than 1 nm (Fig. 2C). High resolution TEM results showed that the lattice spacing of Au@GQD was 0.235 nm, which is consistent with the gold (1 1 1) crystal plane (Fig. 2D). The mixed solution did not change color without irradiation, whereas the gold chloride irradiated with UV was still limpid. Only the GQD and gold chloride mixed solution presented wine after irradiation (Fig. S1). As the irradiation time increased, the color of the solution became darker and darker, which was directly related to the diameter of gold nanosphere (Figs. S2 and S3). Traditional gold nanosphere had no absorption in NIR region (Fig. 2E). However, Au/DOX@GQD-FA had stronger absorption in the NIR region than Au, which enabled it to work during PAI and PTT (Fig. 2F, insert). There was typical characteristic absorption of DOX at the corresponding wavelength position after loading DOX, which demonstrated that the drug had been loaded successful. Due to the GQD used here was Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot, in its preparation, citric acid was used as carbon source and urea could introduce amino group onto GQD surface, FA was immobilized onto GQD through amide reaction so that the targeting probe was obtained. The absorption spectrum of Au@GQD-FA showed that there was typical FA absorption peak at 280 nm, which suggested the successful connection of FA to probe (Fig. 2F). Five corresponding electron diffraction patterns were clearly observed during 20–90° scanning using X-ray diffraction (XRD), which belonged to 111, 200, 220, 222 and 311 reflections (JCPDS 04-0784) of cubic metallic gold, further demonstrating the crystalline structure of Au. Additionally, the dynamic light scattering results showed that the probe size was mainly distributed within the range of 32–68 nm (Fig. 2H), which is consistent with the TEM results. After coupling FA and loading DOX, its zeta potential changed from −10.83 ± 0.42 mV to −24.85 ± 0.84 mV, which could effectively improve the stability of probe (Fig. 2I). After loading DOX, the surface of Au@GQD-FA dropped to more negatively charged. It was considered that in the process of coupling folic acid, the pH was generally adjusted over 8, which was above the pKa of DOX (7.35) [29], causing DOX negatively charged. The size and zeta potential of the probe preserved at 37 °C had no obvious changes within 28 days, further highlighting the stability of probe (Fig. 2J). The stability of probe has been studied in RPMI and RPMI + FBS culture medium as simulated blood, it could be seen that there was no obvious change in 9 d (Fig. S6), indicating that the probe had enough stability in blood.

In recent years, a superior drug-loading capacity of graphene had been widely recognized [30]. On the probe, GQD could effectively take advantage of this drug loading capability. Different masses of DOX were added to 5 mL Au@GQD-FA (0.2 mg/mL, pH of 7.4). The result showed that, with the increase in mass, the loading efficiency gradually increased. However, the encapsulation efficiency gradually declined with the increase in mass of DOX (Fig. 2K). The final loading and encapsulation efficiencies were found to be 94.39 ± 0.39% and 9.44 ± 0.04% for 100 mg DOX in 1 g of Au@GQD-FA, respectively. For the proposed probe, two kinds of control methods were used to release DOX. One was the low pH, while the other was the photothermal process after NIR laser irradiation. The amount of released DOX was 44.55 ± 3.06% after 72 h at the pH of 5.4, indicating significant difference (p < 0.01) compared to the corresponding values of 21.07 ± 3.74% and 19.87 ± 1.41% for pH values of 7.4 and 10.4, respectively (Fig. 2L). It was conjectured that DOX was conjugated on GQD surface through π-π bond [31,32]. Low pH could increase the hydrophilicity and solubility of DOX [32,33]. Meanwhile, acidic environment could also weaken the hydrogen-bonding between DOX and GQD. Both of these factors contributed to the release of DOX [34]. As the probe had good photothermal performance, high temperature could weaken the hydrogen-bonding between DOX and GQD, thus leading to the release of drug. The results showed that DOX was released slowly at the pH of 7.4 (Fig. 2M). However, when the suspension was irradiated with NIR laser, the amount of released DOX increased rapidly. Once the laser was closed, the rate of release of DOX became slow again. The amount of released DOX was 80.76 ± 1.84% after irradiating the system for six times, indicating significant difference (p < 0.01) from the amount released with no irradiation. Because of the acidic environment at the tumor site, the pH-triggered drug release property and the good photothermal effect laid good foundations for drug controlled release in tumor. Furthermore, double-controlled modal could increase the amount of drug released and achieve better therapeutic effect. It was worth noticing that the amount of drug released increased to 57.80 ± 1.42% after 10 min of irradiation after 12 h storage at the pH of 7.4. This value was higher than that at the pH of 5.4 after 72 h, indicating that the NIR-triggered drug release was better than the pH-triggered drug release.

Traditional gold nanospheres do not show any absorption in the NIR region, and therefore, do not exhibit any photothermal effect. However, Au@GQD showed strong absorption in the NIR region. The results showed that, with the increase in laser’s power density, the temperature of Au@GQD (1 mg/mL) also increased. When different concentrations of Au@GQD were irradiated using NIR laser (1 W/cm2), the temperature increased up to higher values with the increase in concentration. When the concentration was 0.5 mg/mL, the temperature increased to 31.9 °C and reached the value of 51.4 °C after 5 min of irradiation, which was high enough to kill the tumor cells. Comparatively, when the mass of GQD coated on 2 mg Au@GQD was 0.5 mg, there was no significant photothermal effect for this concentration of GQD (Fig. 3 C), suggesting that the photothermal effect of probe was mainly coming from Au. The temperature change of probe under several laser irradiation cycles showed that Au@GQD had good photo-thermal stability (Fig. 3E), and the value of τ was 337.26 s (Fig. 3F). According to the equation
η =
hs
T max
-
T surr
-
Q Dis
I(1-
10
-A 808
)
[35], where hs = mc/τ, Q Dis = hs × (T max of water-T surr), m was the mass of solution (g), c was the specific heat capacity of water (J/g·°C), I was the laser power (W), and A 808 was the absorption value at 808 nm. Finally, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was calculated to be 51.31%. The results showed that Au@GQD had excellent photo-thermal conversion, which is very useful for use in PTT of tumor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeted dual-mode imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The probe also showed good abilities of PAI and CT imaging. PAI and CT were determined using in-house built systems [36]. PAI results showed that increasing Au@GQD concentration would result in strengthening of PA signal with a good linear relationship (Fig. 4 A). The CT imaging results showed good linear relationship between the probe concentration and HU value, whereas the ability of imaging quality was twice as iodine under the same conditions (Fig. 4B). The results demonstrated that the probe had excellent dual-modal imaging capability besides the photothermal effect.

The efficient entry of nanoparticles in cells is an essential requirement for drug-loading nanocarriers. When the nanoparticles were modified using targeting ligand, the probe would recognize and combine receptor on the targeted cell, could trigger receptor-mediated endocytosis and enhance the probe uptake. As FA receptor was overexpressed on HeLa cell surface with the opposite effect on A549 cell [37], positive and negative cells were adopted by them, respectively. In the experiments, positive probe was adopted by Au@GQD-FA, while negative probe was represented by Au@GQD without FA. HeLa and A549 cells were incubated using Au@GQD-FA and Au@GQD, respectively. They were enforced to CT and PAI after the individual probes were removed. There was a macroscopic difference between them (Fig. S4). The PAI results showed that HeLa cell had the highest signal using Au@GQD-FA. Based on the T-test results, the photoacoustic (PA) signal of positive cell, incubated with the positive probe, was significantly stronger than other groups (p < 0.01) (Fig. 4C and D). The CT results were consistent with the PAI results, showing that the positive cell with positive probe exhibited the highest HU value (Fig. 4E). These results indicated that Au@GQD-FA could specifically recognize HeLa cell rather than the A549 cell and also showed excellent targeting ability.

Two kinds of cells were incubated using two kinds of probes in the confocal capsule. The cells were cleaned thrice with PBS to remove free probe after 4 h. After the laser irradiation, DOX fluorescence was only observed in HeLa incubated with positive probe instead of the other groups (Fig. 4F). This showed that only the HeLa cell incubated with Au/DOX@GQD-FA could specifically bind to FA receptor. Then, after irradiation, DOX was released into the nucleus from probe through phagocytosis into cell. This result also showed that the probe had excellent targeting and drug releasing abilities.

The toxicity of the probe is key to its application. The two kinds of cells were incubated for 24 h with Au@GQD-FA at different concentrations. The MTT results indicated that, when the probe concentration reached 250 μg/mL, the 79.03 ± 4.50% of the HeLa cells still survived (Fig. 5 A), showing that Au@GQD-FA presented perfect biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Meanwhile, the survival rate of HeLa cells was lower than A549′s (81.02 ± 9.06%), because the amount of Au@GQD-FA penetrating the A549 cell through phagocytosis was less than that for HeLa cell, which led to higher toxicity to HeLa than the A549 cell.

The therapeutic effect of Au/DOX@GQD-FA for HeLa was estimated using MTT assay (Fig. 5B). Since the probe contained constant drug in unit concentration, DOX concentration was used in the following experiments to calibrate the amount of probes added for different treatments unless otherwise noted. The results indicated that laser irradiation presented hardly any effect on cell viability. After treatment with Au@GQD-FA only, the death rate was low, which was consistent with the results obtained from cytotoxicity experiments. And Au@GQD with laser irradiation couldn’t cause large number of cell death, indicating that only a small amount of negative probe can enter the cell. However, the cell viability was significantly reduced after irradiation due to superior photothermal conversion effect of Au@GQD-FA. The probe could kill cancer cell under laser irradiation through effective PTT. The lethality of DOX for cancer cells increased with the increase in concentration of DOX. When DOX concentration reached 6 µg/mL, the cell survival rate dropped to 29.28 ± 0.66%, whereas the effect was similar for DOX and laser irradiation, and it was better than that for Au/DOX@GQD-FA with the same DOX concentration without irradiation. Although DOX could be released from probe in acidic environment, however, it was obvious that DOX loaded in Au/DOX@GQD-FA required a period of time to be released by the acid response in the absence of irradiation (25.87 ± 2.43% after 24 h at pH 5.4, Fig. 2L). It was understandable that the amount of released DOX during total 24 h incubation was not enough to kill plenty of cell, resulting in low cytotoxicity of Au/DOX@GQD-FA to HeLa cell without irradiation. The release process needed time, due to which, its killing effect to cancer cells was not as good as that of the free drug. However, the lethal effect of Au/DOX@GQD-FA to cancer cell significantly increased after irradiation. When the concentration of probe was 6 µg/mL, the cell survival rate was about 17.54 ± 3.53%, indicating significant difference from that of Au@GQD-FA with laser irradiation (26.35 ± 3.62%) (p < 0.01). The heat resulting from the NIR irradiation enhanced the release of DOX from probe, and the cancer cells were killed more readily by chemo-photothermal combined therapy. These results demonstrated that the combined therapy of PTT and drug was better than the single mode (individual) treatment.

And its influence on hemolysis of red blood cell was also investigated. The results showed that red blood cell had a hemolysis ratio of less than 2.03% after being incubated with Au@GQD at a maximum concentration (400 µg/mL) for 8 h (Fig. 5C), indicating that the probe has excellent blood compatibility.

The targeting ability of the probe could be verified using the PTT effect. When HeLa cells were incubated with Au@GQD-FA and irradiated with laser for 5 min, calcein acetoxymethyl ester (calcein-AM) and propidium iodide (PI) staining showed that the cell presented green fluorescence in non-irradiated area, while red fluorescence was observed in the irradiated region (Fig. 5C). Calcein-AM was living cell dye having green fluorescence, while PI could stain dead cells with red fluorescence [38]. If HeLa cells were only incubated with Au@GQD-FA, the cells presented bright green fluorescence. In order to exclude the disturbance of laser, HeLa cells were irradiated using laser without the probe. Furthermore, bright green fluorescence of cells showed that the photothermal effect of Au@GQD-FA resulted in cell death (Fig. S5). HeLa cells were incubated with Au@GQD to investigate the active targeting ability of the probe. The results indicated that the cells still presented green fluorescence after irradiation, showing that the negative Au@GQD probe could not perfectly recognize HeLa. In the control groups, regardless of any of the circumstances mentioned above, A549 with low FA receptor exhibited bright green fluorescence at all the times. The results indicated that HeLa cells could only be killed by irradiation after incubation with Au@GQD-FA, which could prove that Au@GQD-FA presented excellent active targeting and PTT ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo dual-mode imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy
ce:section.ce:para: PAI and CT were carried out in vivo. It was found that the probe reached the maximal accumulation at the tumor site after 8 h of injection (Fig. S7) when it was introduced into mice through caudal vein. In order to ensure treatment effect, intratumor injection was adopted finally. It could be seen that after the probe was injected in the tumor, strong PA signals appeared after 744 nm laser irradiation at the tumor site (Fig. 6 A). The CT results showed that the probe had great CT imaging capability (Fig. 6B). The experimental results showed that the probe presented very good application prospects because of its pre-eminent dual-modal imaging capability.

Encouraged by the high PTT efficacy and excellent killing effect for tumor cells by the probe when used in vitro, the chemo-photothermal combined therapy was implemented in vivo. The results showed that, after PBS and DOX were intratumorally injected in mice, the temperature of tumor site increased by only about 10 °C after 10 min of laser irradiation (Fig. 6C and D), which was inadequate for the apoptosis of cancer cells. Contrastingly, the temperature of tumor site grew up to 40 °C and reached 62 °C after injection of Au@GQD-FA and Au/DOX@GQD-FA with irradiation. This heat was enough to kill the cancer cells (Fig. 6C and D). The weight of mice and tumor sizes were monitored every other day after a series of therapies. In the experiments, there was no obvious weight loss, demonstrating that the injection did not cause obvious side effect and toxicity to animals (Fig. 6E). After treatment, one mice of the five was selected randomly to take photograph and record changes in the tumors (Fig. 7 ). Tumor volumes in groups of PBS, Au@GQD-FA or PBS with irradiation increased rapidly during the experiments, demonstrating that the tumor could not be killed by laser irradiation alone or Au@GQD-FA. If the mice were injected with DOX, Au/DOX@GQD-FA without laser irradiation or DOX with laser irradiation, the tumors still grew, though the growth rates dropped significantly (p < 0.05) because the existence of DOX could kill tumor cells (Fig. 6F). It was a pity that these pure chemotherapies were not enough to kill the tumor thoroughly. After PTT of Au@GQD-FA and Au/DOX@GQD-FA, there were black scars left at the tumor sites, demonstrating the superior PTT performances of the two probes (Fig. 7). Interestingly, after 8 d of irradiation, cancer relapse appeared in Au@GQD-FA group, and the recurrence rate was 60%. The cancer volume began to increase rapidly. The average volume was close to DOX group after 20 d (Fig. 6E). As a contrast, the tumor sites in Au/DOX@GQD-FA group with laser irradiation went through necrosis and scab, while the skin finally recovered (Fig. 7). Just one mouse was relapsed after 18 d, whereas the tumor grew very slowly. This was significantly different from other groups after 20 d (p < 0.01), demonstrating that PTT or drug could not completely kill cancerous cells, though the effect was evidently improved after combined therapy (Fig. 6E). It should be noted that, the black scar on tumor of Au/DOX@GQD-FA without laser group might be due to the fact that tumor grew too fast and broke the skin. In accordance with the requirement for animal ethics, when the aggregate tumor burden became bigger than 1 cm in diameter, the tumor would impede vital activities, including eating, urination, defecation or ambulation. Therefore, at this stage, the mice should be euthanized. On this basis, the survival rate of different treatment groups showed that the chemo-photothermal combined therapy was more efficacious than the single, individual treatment methods (Fig. 6G).

In order to further ensure the killing efficacy of Au/DOX@GQD-FA for cancer, histologic section of cancer was implemented after 6 h of different therapies (Fig. 7). Obviously, H&E staining showed that no necrosis appeared in PBS, PBS combined with laser, and Au@GQD-FA groups. In Au/DOX@GQD-FA group, only slight necrosis occurred. In the DOX and DOX combined with laser groups, there was some necrosis. Evident cancer necrosis happened in Au@GQD-FA and Au/DOX@GQD-FA groups with laser irradiation. The latter necrosis was more serious. These results demonstrated that Au/DOX@GQD-FA presented great combined therapeutic effect on cancer after irradiation.

The biodistribution of Au/DOX@GQD-FA in mice has been studied (Fig. 8 A). It was found that a large amount of probe was enriched in the liver and spleen of mouse after 1 h of tail vein injection. The probe was found to gradually enter into tumor, due to good ability of targeting, reaching its maximum concentration in tumor at 12 h. After that, the probe began to be metabolized by organism and almost disappeared after 72 h.

In order to confirm probe’s safety in vivo, blood biochemical values of mice were collected after injection (Fig. 8B–I). The results showed that no evident difference occurred in white blood cell (WBC), red blood cell (RBC), platelet (PLT) and hemoglobin (HGB) between the probe and PBS control groups, demonstrating that the probe did not produce irreversible harm to mice. Besides, its influence on liver and kidney functions was studied using alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP). The results showed that both the experimental and control groups’ blood biochemical values changed after 1 d of injection, after which, they returned to normal levels after 3 d. The short increases resulted from the adoption of anesthetics, which might affect liver enzyme activity and induce provisional increase in AST and ALT in blood. Furthermore, BUN and ALP temporarily declined after the mice anesthesia due to nutritional deficiency [39]. All of these indices restored to normal ranges within 7 d, indicating that the probe exhibited very good biosafety.

Beside blood analysis, its effect on major organs was also studied. Comparing two groups’ H&E staining results at 6 h, 1 d, 3 d, 7 d and 15 d after injection (Fig. 8J), it was found that no evident change occurred in the tissue structures of kidney, liver, lung, spleen, small intestine and heart. The structure of nucleus was clear and no obvious cell necrosis appeared, indicating that the probe hardly presented any side-effect and toxicity to these organs, further highlighting its superior biosafety.

In this paper, safe multi-functional nanoprobe Au/DOX@GQD-FA for photoacoustic/computed tomography imaging and chemo-photothermal combined therapy was synthesized using a simple one-step method. Both the in vitro and in vivo results showed that the probe had good biocompatibility and low toxicity, and could achieve targeted dual-modal imaging of tumor with overexpressed folic acid receptor. Furthermore, it could also reach the chemo-photothermal combined therapy of cancer through controlled drug release using near-infrared laser and acidic environment of tumor. Additionally, the treatment effect was significantly superior to individual modes. Therefore, the probe presented good application prospects for tumor diagnosis and therapy.

FA, urea and citric acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Chloroauric acid was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Calcein acetoxymethyl ester (calcein-AM), propidium iodide (PI), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) and N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Aladdin Chemicals Co., Ltd., China. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was obtained from Sangon Biotech., Co., Ltd., China. 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Ltd., China. 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from AMRESCO Inc., USA. All reagents were used directly without further purification.

The instruments used in experiments included Nano-ZS90 nanometer size meter (Malvern, UK), UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), EasIR-9 thermal imager (Wuhan Guide Infrared Co., Ltd., China), X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands), concentrator plus (Eppendorf, Germany), MDL-III-808–2.5 W laser (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Tech., Co., Ltd., China), IX71 fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan), Tecnai G20 U-Twin high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, The Netherlands), FV1000 laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan), WFX-200 atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Beijing Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument Co.,Ltd., China), CT and PAI systems were built in-house and named in vivo animal CT imaging system and high resolution photoacoustic imaging system.

0.53 g citric acid and 1.35 g urea were dissolved in 12 mL ultrapure water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then, the solution was transferred into 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, heated to 160 °C in an electric muffle furnace, and maintained at this temperature for 12 h. After cooling, dialysis bag with the molecular weight of 500 Da was used to remove the unreacted reagents. Ultrapure water was replaced at least 10 times.

2.0 mg gold chloride and 1.5 mg N-GQD were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. The solution was irradiated using a 300 W UV lamp for 1 min. After centrifuging at 8000 rad/min for 10 min and after abandoning the supernatant, Au@GQD was obtained by resuspending the precipitate. In this paper, the mass of probe was referred to as the mass of Au without special instruction.

57.51 mg EDC, 34.53 mg NHS and 13.24 mg FA were dissolved in 15 mL MES. After stirring for 1.5 h, the pH was changed over 8. Then, 1 mg Au@GQD was added to the solution, and stirred for 18 h. After similar purification as explained above, Au@GQD-FA was obtained and stored in dark.

100 µg DOX and 1 mg Au@GQD-FA were dissolved in 5 mL PBS and stirred for 18 h while avoiding the light. After similar purification steps as explained above, Au/DOX@GQD-FA was obtained and stored in dark.

200 µL suspensions with different concentration probes (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 mg/mL) were added in ELISA plates, and then, irradiated for 5 min using 808 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2). The temperature was recorded using a thermal camera. The probe of 1.0 mg/mL was illuminated using 808 nm laser with different power densities (0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 W/cm2) for 5 min in the same way. Five parallel experiments were conducted for each group. 1 mL probe (0.2 mg/mL) was put in the quartz cuvette, and then, irradiated using 808 nm laser with 0.8 W for 10 min. After closing the laser, the temperature was recorded, and the conversion efficiency was calculated based on the method reported earlier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PAI and CT evaluation of probe
ce:section.ce:para: Different Au@GQD-FA suspensions (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 and 40 mg/mL) were put in the PCR tube. CT imaging was conducted using an in-house built CT system. Matlab was use for reconstructing the image and reading the values. Different Au@GQD-FA suspensions (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mg/mL) were injected in the flexible pipes. PAI was measured at 744 nm using a 120 nJ laser using an in-house built PA system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation efficiency, loading efficiency and release rate of Au/DOX@GQD-FA
ce:section.ce:para: Au@GQD-FA and DOX were dispersed in PBS in different proportions and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The supernatant was abandoned after centrifuging (8000 rad/min, 10 min) and the procedure was repeated several times until the color disappeared. Then, the supernatant was gathered to calculate the mass of DOX by examining the absorption at 480 nm. The encapsulation efficiency was given by: (massDOX-massDOX in supernatant)/massDOX × 100%, while the loading efficiency was represented by: (massDOX-massDOX in supernatant)/massprobe × 100%.

For pH-triggered drug release, Au/DOX@GQD-FA (0.6 mg) was dispersed in 3 mL PBS (pH = 5.4, 7.4 and 10.4, respectively) for three groups, and put in the dialysis bags with 500 Da molecular weight. The pH of dialysate was also adjusted to corresponding value. The mass of DOX in dialysate was measured at a predetermined time interval. The release rate of DOX was calculated using the expression: massreleased DOX/massloaded DOX × 100%.

For NIR-triggered drug release, Au/DOX@GQD-FA (0.6 mg) was dispersed in 1 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) for two groups, and put in test tubes at 37 °C with water bath for 72 h. At a constant time interval, one was taken to centrifuge (8000 rad/min, 10 min). The precipitate was resuspended using PBS, and then, irradiated for 10 min using 808 nm laser. After centrifuging again, fresh PBS was added, and the suspension was stored at 37 °C. Six irradiations were carried out in 72 h. The DOX in the supernatant was collected for detection after centrifuging. Another group was treated the same way, however without irradiation. Three parallel experiments were adopted.

The logarithmic phase HeLa and A549 cells were seeded in 96-well plate at 104/well for 6 groups with 5 parallels. Au@GQD-FA suspensions (0, 31.3, 62.5, 125, 250 and 500 μg/mL) were injected. 20 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was followed and maintained for 4 h after 24 h of incubation. Then, the medium was removed and 150 μL DMSO was injected. After shaking the plate for 15 min, the absorbance was measured at 490 nm to calculate the cell survival rate.

One milliliter of blood sample obtained from mice was diluted with 4 mL of PBS; after that, red blood cell was separated from the serum by centrifugation at 3000 rad/min for 10 min. After being washed for at least five times, the red blood cell suspension was diluted with 5 mL of PBS. After that, 200 μL of the diluted cell suspension was mixed with 300 μL of PBS (negative control), ultrapure water (positive control) or at different concentrations of Au@GQD (25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL). After incubation at 37 °C for 1, 4 or 8 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rad/min for 10 min. The supernatant was subsequently added into a 96-well plate, which was then subjected to measurement of absorbance using a microplate reader. The hemolysis percentage was calculated by the following equation: hemolysis ratio (%) = [(A sample − A control(-))/(A control(+) − A control(−))] × 100%.

Au/DOX@GQD-FA aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was added into RPMI and RPMI + FBS culture medium and exposed to 37 °C under air. They were sampled every 2 d to measure size, zeta potential and particle dispersion index (PDI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro PAI, CT and laser scanning confocal image
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa and A549 cells were inoculated in cell culture flask for 24 h and treated with Au@GQD or Au@GQD-FA (250 μg/mL) for 4 h. After removing the culture medium, PBS was used to wash the cells thrice to get rid of free probe. 2.5% glutaraldehyde was added to immobilize the cells, and then, the cells were collected for PAI and CT after scraping with cell scraper carefully. The HeLa and A549 cells were seeded into confocal capsule at 8 × 105/well and treated with Au@GQD or Au@GQD-FA (62.5 μg/mL) for 4 h. After removing the culture medium, PBS was used to wash the cells thrice to get rid of free probe. After irradiation using 808 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min, the cells were cultured for another 4 h. The cells were immobilized with 2.5% glutaraldehyde and dyed using DAPI, after which, they were observed using laser scanning confocal microscopy.

The HeLa and A549 cells were seeded in 24-well plate at 2 × 105/well and treated with Au@GQD or Au@GQD-FA (250 μg/mL) for 4 h. After removing the culture medium, PBS was used to wash the cells thrice to get rid of free probe. Then, the cells were irradiated using 808 nm laser (1.5 W/cm2) for 10 min. Calcein-AM and PI were used to stain the cells for 5 min, after which, they were observed using inverted optical fluorescence microscope.

MTT assay was used to test the therapeutic effect of Au/DOX@GQD-FA on cancer cells. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plate at 104/well and cultured for 24 h. The cells were treated with free DOX, Au@GQD, Au@GQD-FA and Au/DOX@GQD-FA with different concentrations (0, 2, 4, 6 and 8 μg/mL for DOX; 0, 20.83, 41.67, 62.50 and 83.33 μg/mL corresponding to Au) for 4 h and treated with 1.5 W/cm2 NIR laser (10 min) or without any treatment. After incubation at 37 °C for another 20 h, 20 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was injected and maintained for 4 h. Then, 150 μL DMSO was injected after removing the medium. After shaking the plate for 15 min, the absorbance was measured at 490 nm to calculate the cell survival rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo PAI and CT imaging
ce:section.ce:para: BALB/c nude mice (male, SPF, 4 weeks) were inoculated with HeLa cell. Once the cancer volume reached the value of 60–100 mm3, Au/DOX@GQD-FA (2 mg/mL) was injected. The mice were anesthetized using 10 µL/g urethane. PAI was carried out using in-house built imaging system using 744 nm laser at 120 nJ. The CT imaging was measured using in-house built CT system. Matlab was used for image reconstruction. All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Experimental Ethics Committee of Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China.

When the tumor volume reached 60–100 mm3, HeLa tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 8 groups (6 mice/group), and 100 μL of different probes were intratumorally injected: (I) PBS, (II) PBS + laser, (III) DOX, (IV) DOX + laser, (V) Au@GQD-FA, (VI) Au@GQD-FA + laser, (VII) Au/DOX@GQD-FA and (VIII) Au/DOX@GQD-FA + laser. Additionally, the 808 nm laser was adopted to treat even numbered groups for 10 min at 2 W/cm2. The temperature of tumor site was recorded using a thermal camera. The concentration of each probe was 2 mg/mL, while the concentration of DOX was 192 μg/mL. One mouse was chosen from each group to take the tumor out for H&E staining and histopathological examination after 6 h treatment. The changes in weight and tumor volume of remaining five mice in each group were recorded every other day. When the diameter of tumor reached 1 cm, the mouse was regarded being dead.

Fifty BALB/c mice (male, SPF, 4 weeks) were randomly divided into 2 groups. One group was injected with Au/DOX@GQD-FA through tail vein, while the other was treated with PBS. Then, 300 μL blood was collected at 6 h, 1 d, 3 d, 7 d and 15 d, 200 μL for liver enzyme analysis (aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP)). Additionally, another 100 μL blood was collected for blood analysis (red blood cell (RBC), white blood cell (WBC), platelet (PLT) and hemoglobin (HGB)). The major organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and small intestine were collected simultaneously. After they were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, dehydrated, sliced and stained with H&E, the histopathologic examination was performed using microscope. Another 5 mice without any treatment were used as blanks. Their blood and main organs were collected for analysis and histopathologic examination.

After the tumor volume reached 60–100 mm3, HeLa tumor-bearing mice were injected through caudal vein with 100 µL of probe (Au; dose: 20 mg/kg). After that, they were killed at different time points (1, 6, 12, 24 and 72 h; n = 3). Their main organs and tumor were weighed and ground, and were thereafter dissolved in aqua regia for the determination of Au content by atomic absorption spectrophotometer.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0700402), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81771878), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Hust: 2016YXMS253, 2017KFXKJC002, 2018KFYXKJC048). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.03.035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoacoustic tomography: in vivo imaging from organelles to organs
Semiconducting oligomer nanoparticles as an activatable photoacoustic probe with amplified brightness for in vivo imaging of pH
Initial results of in vivo non-invasive cancer imaging in the human breast using near-infrared photoacoustics
Core-shell Pd@Au nanoplates as theranostic agents for in-vivo photoacoustic imaging, CT imaging, and photothermal therapy
Ultrafast synthesis of ultrasmall poly(vinylpyrrolidone)-protected bismuth nanodots as a multifunctional theranostic agent for in vivo dual-modal CT/photothermal-imaging-guided photothermal therapy
Reverse microemulsion-mediated synthesis of Bi2S3-QD@SiO2-PEG for dual modal CT-fluorescence imaging in vitro and in vivo
Enhanced performance of light-controlled conductive switching in hybrid cuprous oxide/reduced graphene oxide (Cu2O/rGO) nanocomposites
Compact plasmonic blackbody for cancer theranosis in the near-infrared II window
Cell membrane coated semiconducting polymer nanoparticles for enhanced multimodal cancer phototheranostics
Temperature-correlated afterglow of a semiconducting polymer nanococktail for imaging-guided photothermal therapy
Semiconducting polymer nanoenzymes with photothermic activity for enhanced cancer therapy
Exceptionally high payload of doxorubicin in hollow gold nanospheres for near-infrared light-triggered drug release
Targeting mesoporous silica-encapsulated gold nanorods for chemo-photothermal therapy with near-infrared radiation
Amphiphilic semiconducting polymer as multifunctional nanocarrier for fluorescence/photoacoustic imaging guided chemo-photothermal therapy
Development of organic semiconducting materials for deep-tissue optical imaging, phototherapy and photoactivation
Formation of gold nanostar-coated hollow mesoporous silica for tumor multimodality imaging and photothermal therapy

In vivo computed tomography/photoacoustic imaging and NIR-triggered chemo-photothermal combined therapy based on a gold nanostar-, mesoporous silica-, and thermosensitive liposome-composited nanoprobe
Light-triggered assembly of gold nanoparticles for photothermal therapy and photoacoustic imaging of tumors in vivo
Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-surface modified P25 nanocomposites for photocatalytic degradation of p-nitrophenol and acid violet 43
The synergistic role of carbon quantum dots for the improved photocatalytic performance of Bi2MoO6
Fluorescent graphene quantum dots as traceable, pH-sensitive drug delivery systems
Immobilizing water-soluble graphene quantum dots with gold nanoparticles for a low potential electrochemiluminescence immunosensor
Two-step synthesis of Ag@GQD hybrid with enhanced photothermal effect and catalytic performance
Nanoparticle synthesis via the photochemical polythiol process
Targeting N-doped graphene quantum dot with high photothermal conversion efficiency for dual-mode imaging and therapy in vitro
Cytostatic drugs in environmental samples: an update on the extraction and determination procedures
Stimuli responsive drug delivery systems based on nano-graphene for cancer therapy
Controlled release of drug from folate-decorated and graphene mediated drug delivery system: synthesis, loading efficiency, and drug release response
Supramolecular chemistry on water-soluble carbon nanotubes for drug loading and delivery
PEGylated nanographene oxide for delivery of water-insoluble cancer drugs
Targeted killing of cancer cells in vivo and in vitro with EGF-directed carbon nanotube-based drug delivery
Supra-(carbon nanodots) with a strong visible to near-infrared absorption band and efficient photothermal conversion
Assessing the effects of norepinephrine on single cerebral microvessels using optical-resolution photoacoustic microscope
Folate-conjugated polymer micelles for active targeting to cancer cells: preparation, in vitro evaluation of targeting ability and cytotoxicity
A multifunctional targeting probe with dual-mode imaging and photothermal therapy used in vivo
The influence of modified Pluronic F127 copolymers with higher phase transition temperature on arsenic trioxide-releasing properties and toxicity in a subcutaneous model of rats
